text: The surgeon attempts to resect and remove a lymph node that obstructs the view of the cystic duct and artery during the dissection of a difficult gallbladder.
The cystic duct and cystic artery are clearly exposed in the surgical field, allowing for precise identification and further dissection.
The surgeon prepares to take the cystic artery first, aiming to enhance exposure for the dissection of the cystic duct and surrounding ducts.
In the tight surgical space, the surgeon uses scissors to navigate and manipulate tissues, ensuring precise dissection.
The surgeon uses the pro grasp instrument to retract and elevate the liver, allowing the gallbladder to retract inferiorly. The dome down technique is initiated from the top of the gallbladder.
The surgeon visually identifies the location of the vessels in the surgical field, with clear exposure of these structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection down to the mesentery pedicle, preparing to clip it at the end of the procedure.
The surgeon inspects the gallbladder fossa, ensuring a clear view of the area after the gallbladder has been retracted and dissected.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to enhance visualization of the gallbladder and surrounding structures.
The surgeon visually inspects the cystic duct, which remains as the last structure to be addressed in the procedure.
The surgeon utilizes indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence imaging to enhance visualization and guide the dissection process during the robotic cholecystectomy.
